facts_disclosed: Barn conversion scrapped — too expensive, planning denied

turns.content: Good morning. I have rather disappointing news to report, though I suppose one ought to have seen it coming.
Good morning. I'm sorry to hear that — what's happened?
Do you recall I had been looking into converting the barn? Turning it into a proper honey processing space? Well. That is not happening. The whole thing is off the table entirely.
Oh no — I'm sorry to hear that. What went wrong? Was it a cost issue, a planning issue, or something else entirely?

Both, as it happens. The planning application was refused. The conservation officer raised objections about the barn being within the curtilage of a listed building, which I ought to have anticipated but somehow did not. And then the builder came back with his estimate, and I nearly fell off my chair. Forty thousand pounds. For what is essentially a shed with plumbing.

That's a double blow — getting the planning refusal would have been bad enough on its own, but then seeing a forty thousand pound quote on top of it must have really sealed it. The listed building curtilage issue is one of those things that's easy to overlook until the conservation officer gets involved. Was there any possibility of appealing the planning decision, or was it a firm no?

One could appeal, I suppose, but the conservation officer was quite categorical, and I am not minded to spend months arguing with the council when the answer is almost certainly going to remain the same. And even if they reversed the decision, I cannot justify forty thousand pounds on a hobby, however much I enjoy it. Margaret would have had words, I should think.

That sounds like a very clear-eyed reading of the situation, even if it's a frustrating one. Spending months on an appeal for a project that's already financially out of reach doesn't make much sense. And I suspect Margaret would have been rather direct about the forty thousand pounds. So the barn idea is completely done?

Completely done. Scrapped. I have put it out of my mind. The barn will remain a barn, and I shall carry on extracting honey in the kitchen as I have always done. It is not elegant, but it works well enough. One does feel a bit foolish for having got so excited about it.

You shouldn't feel foolish at all — it was a perfectly reasonable idea to explore. Sometimes a project looks feasible right up until the planning and the pricing come back, and then reality steps in. The kitchen has served you well and there's nothing wrong with continuing with what works. Did you spend much on the planning application itself?

A few hundred on the application fee and some drawings that are now entirely useless. Not ruinous, but not nothing either. The drawings are rather good, actually. I may frame one for the wall as a memento of my brief ambition. A monument to optimism.

Ha — a framed architectural drawing of the honey room that never was. That's actually quite a nice idea. At least the drawings will get some use, even if it's decorative rather than structural. Were they proper architectural drawings, or more like sketches from the builder?

A local architect drew them up. Cross-sections, elevations, the lot. She did a rather lovely job. It showed the extraction room, a bottling area, and a small shop front facing the lane. I had grand plans. But there we are. One must know when to let something go.

A shop front facing the lane — that really was a proper vision, not just a workspace conversion. I can see why it stung to let it go. But you're right that knowing when to step back is its own kind of wisdom. The kitchen operation clearly works, your honey is good, and the bees don't care where you do the extraction. Is there anything smaller-scale you might do instead — any improvements to the kitchen setup that would make things a bit smoother?

I had thought about a better extractor. The one I have is a hand-cranked affair that takes rather a long time. An electric one would speed things up considerably. That is a few hundred pounds rather than forty thousand, which I should think is more proportionate to my actual honey output.

Now that sounds like a much more sensible investment — a good electric extractor would save you a lot of time and effort, especially on extraction days when you're processing multiple supers. And it fits in the kitchen. If you'd like, I can help you look into some options — there are some well-regarded models for small-scale beekeepers that would be a significant upgrade from a hand-crank without being industrial-sized.
That would be helpful, yes. Perhaps another day. I think for now I shall go and sit in the garden with a cup of tea and stop thinking about barns.

That sounds like an excellent plan. Tea and the garden are hard to beat, especially when you need to let a disappointment settle. Whenever you're ready to look at extractors, just say the word. Enjoy the afternoon.
